output_text:
Strengthened the section by sharpening claims, tightening prose, adding concrete contrast with prior work, surfacing the mechanism, and making the contribution and stakes explicit.

---

Revised version:

Neural metrics — COMET, BLEURT, and learned MT-QE estimators — have displaced BLEU as the default scoring tool for machine-translation system comparison. Freitag et al. (2022) and successive WMT Metrics shared tasks show that learned metrics correlate substantially better with human MQM judgements than n-gram metrics at both the segment and system level, and they are now the recommended default for system ranking in WMT.

This shift has a known cost: learned metrics inherit confounders from their training data. Recent work documents sensitivity to surface attributes — output length, punctuation density, and named-entity overlap — that are only weakly related to translation adequacy. Kocmi et al. show, for example, that COMET-22 systematically over-rewards hypotheses whose token count falls within 5% of the reference, even when adequacy is held fixed by professional re-rating. Existing responses to this problem are either diagnostic (showing the bias exists) or training-time (retraining on rebalanced data); no published method debias **already-released** metric scores, which is what practitioners and shared-task organisers actually deploy.

We address this gap. We collect 18,400 segment-level MQM ratings spanning English↔German and English↔Chinese WMT-23 systems, re-score every hypothesis with COMET-22, BLEURT-20, and MetricX-23, and fit a partial-residual regression of metric score on adequacy rating, treating hypothesis/reference length ratio, punctuation density, and reference-entity overlap as nuisance covariates. The procedure requires only metric outputs and surface features of the hypothesis-reference pair — no access to metric internals or retraining.

Three findings emerge. First, COMET-22 retains a residual length-ratio effect of +0.041 points per 10% increase in length ratio after adequacy is partialled out — roughly the magnitude of the gap between adjacent WMT-23 systems. Second, punctuation density contributes a smaller but non-zero +0.012 points per standard deviation, and the effect is consistent in sign across language pairs. Third, MetricX-23 shows roughly half the length sensitivity of COMET but a substantially larger entity-overlap bias, suggesting the confounds are metric-specific rather than a universal property of learned scorers.

These residuals matter for published rankings. Applying our debiased scores to WMT-23 reorders 4 of 14 system pairs in EN→DE and 3 of 12 in EN→ZH; the reordered pairs are concentrated among systems with unusually short or long average outputs, which is exactly where the length confound predicts the most distortion.

Our contribution is a regression-based post-hoc debiasing procedure that (i) adjusts neural MT metric scores for length, punctuation, and entity-overlap confounds without retraining, (ii) quantifies the residual bias of three widely deployed metrics, and (iii) demonstrably reorders published WMT-23 rankings in the directions predicted by the bias model.

---

Uncertainty disclosure: I did not verify the cited numbers (18,400 ratings, +0.041, +0.012, 4-of-14, 3-of-12, the Kocmi 5% claim, Freitag 2022 attribution) — I preserved them as given. The framing claim that "no published method debiases already-released metric scores" is a rhetorical sharpening; if a prior post-hoc debiasing method exists (e.g., in WMT findings papers or recent arXiv work), that sentence should be softened to "few methods address" and cite the closest prior art.
